moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn two squares forward, immediately claiming central space and freeing both our bishop and queen for future development. This classical move controls the vital d5 and f5 squares, preventing Black from easily establishing pieces there. The e4 advance also opens diagonals for our light-squared bishop to potentially go to c4 or b5, and gives us excellent winning chances in the center. Against many defenses, this leads to sharp tactical play where we can press for an early initiative.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.e4 claims the center, opens lines for the queen and bishop, and controls d5 and f5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black chooses the Pirc Defense setup, a hypermodern approach that allows us to build a strong pawn center while Black develops pieces to active squares before challenging us. This move supports an eventual ...e5 advance and keeps the e5 square under Black's control, while also preparing to fianchetto the dark-squared pawn on g7. The Pirc is a flexible defense that can transpose into various systems like the Modern Defense or even delayed King's Indian setups. Black is essentially saying 'go ahead and take the center, I'll challenge it later from the flanks with my pieces.'
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's Pirc setup allows White a strong center while Black prepares ...e5 and a g7 fianchetto to counter from the flanks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete our classical pawn center with this natural advance, now controlling an impressive amount of central space with our pawn duo on e4 and d4. This move directly challenges Black's control over the e5 square and opens lines for our light-squared bishop and queen. The d4-e4 pawn chain gives us excellent central control and supports aggressive piece development, particularly our knights to f3 and c3. Against the Pirc, this solid central approach is considered one of the most principled ways to meet Black's hypermodern setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 completes the classical pawn center, controlling e5 and opening lines for the bishop and queen.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its ideal square, immediately putting pressure on our e4 pawn and preparing to castle kingside. This is a critical move in the Pirc Defense because the knight from f6 can later jump to excellent squares like d7, h5, or even e8 to support central counterplay with ...e5. The knight also supports Black's plan to fianchetto the dark-squared pawn on g7, creating a powerful battery aimed at our center. From f6, this knight can be a real thorn in our side if we don't develop purposefully.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 pressures e4, supports castling, and can later jump to d7, h5, or e8 for central counterplay.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight settles on c3, supporting the d4 pawn and preparing to reroute toward e2 or g4 depending on Black's setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 is the standard theory move in the Pirc Classical Schlechter Variation.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black fianchettoes the kingside bishop, preparing to aim it along the long diagonal and control key central squares from afar.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: g6 is theory in the Pirc Classical Schlechter Variation.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the knight on f3, reinforcing d4 and keeping options open to support a kingside attack or advance e5 later.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 is the Schlechter Variation of the Pirc Defense Classical line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop completes Black's fianchetto setup, now exerting pressure on the long diagonal and eyeing White's potentially weak light squares.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bg7 is theory in the Pirc Classical Schlechter Variation.

moveAnnotations.annotation: A prophylactic move that prevents Black's knight from jumping to g4 and prepares to secure g3 for the bishop or knight later.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: h3 prevents Ng4 and prepares g4 to gain space on the kingside.
